check: Break the centered-single-column default deliberately somewhere on the page
description: At least one section uses an asymmetric, off-grid, or full-bleed layout rather than every section being a centered max-width column stacked vertically
why_it_matters: A page where literally every section is centered-column-stacked is the single most recognizable 'templated' pattern — one deliberate break in the rhythm signals a real layout decision was made

check: Vary section rhythm — not every section the same height/padding
description: Sections have intentionally different vertical rhythm (some tight, some spacious) rather than uniform padding applied mechanically throughout
why_it_matters: Uniform section padding applied via a single component/token everywhere is efficient but reads as unconsidered — real editorial layouts vary pacing deliberately

check: Use a grid where content genuinely has more than one dimension, not force-fit a list into a grid
description: Grids are used for content with a real 2D relationship (a gallery, a comparison, a dashboard); ordered/sequential content stays in a list or single column
why_it_matters: Forcing sequential content into a 3-column grid because grids look 'more designed' obscures the actual reading order and hurts comprehension

check: Motion has a purpose beyond decoration — conveys state change, spatial relationship, or feedback
description: Every animation on the page can be described in terms of what it communicates (this appeared from here, this state changed, this action was received) — not 'it looked cool'
why_it_matters: Decorative-only motion adds visual noise and performance cost without earning it — this is `ui-ux-pro-max`'s own animation guidance restated, and remains true regardless of how original the rest of the system is

check: At least one micro-interaction feels custom, not the framework/library default
description: A hover state, a button press, a page transition, or a loading state uses timing/easing that's been deliberately tuned rather than left at a UI library's default spring/duration
why_it_matters: Default library transitions (a generic 200ms ease-out on everything) are invisible in isolation but collectively signal 'unmodified template' across a whole product — one deliberately-tuned interaction changes that impression disproportionately

check: Original or genuinely curated photography/illustration over generic stock or default AI-gen look
description: Hero images and key brand moments use photography/illustration with a specific, consistent art direction — not visibly generic stock photos or unedited default-style AI generations
why_it_matters: Generic stock photography (or unstyled AI-generated imagery with no consistent art direction) is one of the fastest ways a page reads as templated, regardless of how original the layout/type/color choices are

check: Apply a consistent visual treatment across all imagery (color grade, crop style, or illustration style)
description: Every image on a page/product shares a deliberate, consistent treatment (a duotone overlay, a specific aspect ratio/crop convention, a consistent illustration line weight) rather than each image looking independently sourced
why_it_matters: Inconsistent imagery treatment (different color temperatures, aspect ratios, and styles side by side) undercuts an otherwise-considered design system more than almost any other single factor

check: Custom or consistently-styled icon set, not mixed icon libraries
description: All icons across the product come from one consistent set/style (stroke width, corner radius, fill style) — never mixed from multiple icon libraries with visibly different visual weights
why_it_matters: Mixed icon styles (a thin-stroke icon next to a filled icon next to a differently-cornered icon) is a subtle but consistent 'unfinished' signal, and is surprisingly common when icons get added ad hoc over time

check: Spacing follows a defined scale (e.g. 4px/8px base), not arbitrary pixel values
description: Every margin/padding value in the codebase traces back to a small, defined spacing scale rather than one-off pixel values chosen per-component
why_it_matters: Arbitrary spacing values accumulate into a visually inconsistent rhythm across a product even when no single instance looks wrong in isolation — a defined scale is what keeps a large product feeling like one system

check: Generous, not cramped, whitespace around key content/CTAs
description: Primary calls-to-action and key content have noticeably more surrounding space than secondary content — whitespace itself is used as an emphasis tool
why_it_matters: Cramming interface elements together to 'fit more' reduces the perceived value/confidence of a product — generous space around what matters most is a cheap, high-impact premium signal

check: Headlines/copy are specific to this product, not generic template copy ('Powerful features for your business')
description: Marketing copy references the product's actual value proposition, actual users, or actual mechanism — not interchangeable placeholder-style copy that could describe any SaaS product
why_it_matters: Generic template copy is as strong a 'templated' signal as generic visual design — copy is part of the design system, not a separate concern handled later

check: Empty states, error states, and edge cases have the same design attention as the happy path
description: Loading states, empty states (no data yet), and error states are explicitly designed, not left as framework defaults or afterthoughts
why_it_matters: These states are disproportionately visible to new users specifically (empty state) and frustrated users specifically (error state) — under-designing exactly the states that matter most for first impressions and trust is a common, costly oversight

check: Hover/focus/active states are visually distinct from each other, not just a generic brightness change
description: Interactive elements have deliberately different treatments for hover vs. focus (keyboard) vs. active (pressed) states, not the same generic opacity/brightness shift applied to all three
why_it_matters: Undifferentiated states make an interface feel less responsive and less crafted — this is also directly connected to `QA029`'s focus-indicator accessibility requirement, which needs a genuinely visible (not just slightly-different) focus state

check: Loading states use a skeleton/shimmer matching the actual content shape, not a generic spinner for everything
description: Content areas show a skeleton loader shaped like the content that will appear (matching card/list/text layout), reserving spinners for short, indeterminate waits
why_it_matters: A generic centered spinner for every loading state (regardless of what's loading or how long) is both a worse UX (no sense of progress/shape) and a visible template signal

check: At least one deliberately unusual/signature element the brand can own (a distinctive cursor, a signature transition, an unusual shape language)
description: The product has one identifiable, repeatable visual/interaction signature that a user would recognize as 'this brand specifically' if shown out of context
why_it_matters: A product with zero distinctive elements — every choice defensible individually as 'good practice' — can still add up to something with no memorable identity; one deliberate signature element addresses this directly

check: Distinctive choices are applied systemically, not as one-off flourishes
description: Whatever distinctive element is chosen (an unusual color, a signature shape, a specific motion style) appears consistently across the product, not as an isolated decoration on just the homepage hero
why_it_matters: A one-off distinctive flourish that doesn't repeat elsewhere reads as decoration bolted onto an otherwise-generic system, rather than as a real design decision — distinctiveness needs to be a system-level commitment to read as intentional

check: Before shipping, ask: 'could this exact page belong to five other competitors with just a logo swap?'
description: A deliberate design-review question applied to any new marketing/brand page before launch — if the honest answer is yes, at least one of layout, color, type, imagery, or copy needs a more specific decision
why_it_matters: This is the single most useful, cheapest distinctiveness check available — it catches genericness that's hard to see from inside the process of building the page incrementally
